comment_text.snp5.10:3.2: Bodhi reads kappī (against the Niddesa) in the sense “manner”. But it seems unlikely Todeyya, or anyone really, would ask whether a sage merely seemed wise. Surely the question must be whether a sage is someone who is still in the process of learning, a question that has been important to the Buddhist traditions and still is today.

translation_text.snp5.10:1.1: “In whom sensual pleasures do not dwell,”
translation_text.snp5.10:1.3: “and for whom there is no craving,
translation_text.snp5.10:1.4: and who has crossed over indecision—
translation_text.snp5.10:1.5: of what kind is their liberation?”
translation_text.snp5.10:2.1: “In whom sensual pleasures do not dwell,”
translation_text.snp5.10:2.3: “and for whom there is no craving,
translation_text.snp5.10:2.4: and who has crossed over indecision—
translation_text.snp5.10:2.5: there is no liberation that is further.”
translation_text.snp5.10:3.1: “Are they free of hope, <j>or are they still in need of hope?
translation_text.snp5.10:3.2: Do they possess wisdom, <j>or are they still forming wisdom?
translation_text.snp5.10:3.3: O Sakyan, elucidate the sage to me,
translation_text.snp5.10:3.4: so that I may understand, All-seer.”
translation_text.snp5.10:4.1: “They are free of hope, <j>they are not in need of hope.
translation_text.snp5.10:4.2: They possess wisdom, <j>they are not still forming wisdom.
translation_text.snp5.10:4.3: That, Todeyya, is how to understand a sage,
translation_text.snp5.10:4.4: one who has nothing, unattached to sensual life.”
